coredata.dc:title: Advanced catalytic performance for the electro-oxidation of methanol enabled by channel-rich Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb nano-pompons
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jinling
Shao, Tao
Meng, Bowen
He, Shijie
Zhang, Qiankun
Zhang, Dongxia
Zhou, Xibin
coredata.dc:description: At present, Pt-based catalysts with controllable compositions, architectures, and crystal facets are still the most prevalent materials for the electro-oxidation of methanol (MOR), which is a compelling reaction in proton exchange membrane fuel cells. Herein, based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs), we report a facial and environment-friendly approach to prepare the Au@GQDs@PtxPb core-shell nano-pompons for the MOR in alkaline solutions. It was noticed that among all the as-synthesized samples, Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb displays an admirable activity, which is as high as 15.14 A mg−1 Pt and almost 38-fold higher than that of commercial Pt/C catalyst. And its resistance to poisoning species and long-term stability are also greatly improved. The improved performance for MOR might be attributed to the channel-rich structure of catalysts with the aid of GQDs, Au, and the electronic effect between Pt and Pb. The results indicate that the reasonable composition and structure of Au@GQDs@PtxPb exhibits efficient MOR, it could be a promising candidate for direct methanol fuel cells.

head.ce:title: Advanced catalytic performance for the electro-oxidation of methanol enabled by channel-rich Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb nano-pompons

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Geography and Environment Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-electrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Geography and Environment Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: At present, Pt-based catalysts with controllable compositions, architectures, and crystal facets are still the most prevalent materials for the electro-oxidation of methanol (MOR), which is a compelling reaction in proton exchange membrane fuel cells. Herein, based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs), we report a facial and environment-friendly approach to prepare the Au@GQDs@PtxPb core-shell nano-pompons for the MOR in alkaline solutions. It was noticed that among all the as-synthesized samples, Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb displays an admirable activity, which is as high as 15.14 A mg−1 Pt and almost 38-fold higher than that of commercial Pt/C catalyst. And its resistance to poisoning species and long-term stability are also greatly improved. The improved performance for MOR might be attributed to the channel-rich structure of catalysts with the aid of GQDs, Au, and the electronic effect between Pt and Pb. The results indicate that the reasonable composition and structure of Au@GQDs@PtxPb exhibits efficient MOR, it could be a promising candidate for direct methanol fuel cells.

ce:section.ce:para: With the expend of fossil fuels and the escalation in environmental contamination, direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) have been considered as one of the most promising power substitutes in industrial, domiciliary, military, and conveyance applications for the merits of relatively high theoretical energy density (6.1 kWh/kg, 4.82 kWh/L), easy storage, less corrosive, quick refueling, and easy biodegradability [1–4]. At the same time, methanol is abundant in source and can be synthesized at a low cost by syngas converted from coal or biomass [5]. And the latest research suggests that methanol can be produced by reduction of CO2, which could lead it to a potential fuel [6]. However, DMFCs are still far from wide-spread commercial viability due to the sluggish kinetics, methanol crossover, and the high cost of the catalyst [7]. Therefore, the exploration of new catalysts with great performance towards MOR is still a challenge.

Nowadays, Pt-based catalysts are acknowledged as the best effective anode materials for the MOR as their outstanding catalytic properties [8]. Even so, the platinum dosage amount (several milligrams) of DMFCs is still much higher and the cost of the catalyst is even 1/2 of the entire fuel cells [9]. More importantly, Pt-based catalysts are easily poisoned by the COads intermediates, which can block the active sites of the catalysts, preventing further reactions from occurring [10–12]. To overcome these problems, tremendous efforts have been devoted to tuning Pt based nano-crystals by various strategies, including control the size, shape, composition, crystal structure, and surface chemistry of the catalytic nanoparticles, as well as the the support and its interaction with the metal. Among them,alloying Pt with a second transition metal including Pd [13], Ru [14], Ni [15], Pb [16], and so on is a commonly efficient strategy to improve the tolerance of COads poisoning due to the electronic effect between Pt and other metals. Among them, Pt-Pb has attracted many interests because the presence of Pb could change the electronic structure of Pt, lower the COads adsorption energy on the surface of the catalyst, and the greater oxy-tropism of Pb can accelerate the oxidation of COads [17–19]. For example, Zhang et al. synthesized the hierarchical Pt/PtxPb core/shell nanowires with dendritic morphology, which exhibited excellent electro-catalytic performance and distinguished tolerance of CO-poisoning [20]. And Kim with his colleagues have successfully fabricated the PtPb hexagonal nanoplates, and they also concluded that the Pb distribution on the PtPb alloy surface aided its high COads tolerance, superior activity, and stability for MOR [21].

Another effective approach is to synthesis a variety of electro-catalysts with desirable nanostructures, such as nanowire, nanotube, hollow, nano-dendritic, core-shell, and so on [22]. Among numerous structures, the core-shell structure is found to be more promising in catalysis, not only they can reduce the usage of Pt, but also can promote the catalytic function of Pt via the bi-functional effect, originating from the strain and ligand effect at the core/shell interfaces. At the present stage, a wide range of core have been investigated including Au, Pd, Pd alloys, Ru, Ir alloys, and other first-row transition metals and their oxides. As stated previously, Au possesses a higher oxidation potential (1.48 V vs. NHE) than Pt, making Au more stable in the electrolytes. And because of this, if Pt shells are attached to Au, the surface redox properties of the Pt shells will be changed, improving stability [23]. This subtle charge transfer also can increase the d-band vacancy of Pt sites, subsequently weakening the binding of COads on Pt sites, facilitating the removal of intermediate CO-like species, and promote the anti-poisoning abilities of Pt catalysts in MOR [24].

The supporting materials are also playing an important role that affects the electrochemical activity and the stability of metallic catalysts for fuel cells [25–27]. Carbon materials containing carbon black [28], graphene [29], carbon fibers [30], carbon quantum dots [31], and grapheme quantum dots [32] have been usually applied as catalyst substrates for their high electrical conductivity and large surface area. Among them, graphene quantum dot (GQDs), the latest addition to the nano‑carbon materials family, have garnered tremendous attention in various fields, such as electro-catalytic, energy conversion, flexible devices, bio-sensing, and therapy. GQDs are unique zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterial, composed of a framework analogous to graphene. Comparing to its 2D counterpart, GQDs are featured by ultra-small particle size (<10 nm), which provides more oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups) and defects on the planar surface, they can serve as highly active sites, it also can effectively prevent metal nanoparticles from aggregation [33]. For instance, Xiao et al. synthesized (M/GQDs)n (M=Au, Ag, Pt) composites via layer-by-layer assembly, which exhibits efficient catalytic ability in MOR and water splitting [34]. Chen et al. prepared Pt/GQDs nanoparticles, which also displayed markedly catalytic stability for oxygen reduction [35]. Transition-metal nanoparticles were also encapsulated into carbon nanotubes (CNTs) or spheres by Yu et al. They found that the stable carbon layer can protect the inner core from the damaging reaction environment and thus is named as chain mail for catalysts, which shows highly durability in a variety of catalytic reactions [36]. Furthermore, it has been reported that GQDs can provide a way of activating water to accelerate the oxidation of COads in MOR [37]. Additionally, the surface of GQDs has abundant oxygen-containing groups (such as -OH, -COOH), which endue them good water solubility and regulate the synthesis of the incompact small dendritic tendrils by the better surface grafting using π–π* conjugation and functional groups, thus further boosting the electro-catalytic performance of the composites [38].

Inspired by the good catalytic activity of PtPb, effect of core-shell structure, and the uniqueness of GQDs, herein, we report a facile strategy to prepare a nano-composite of Au@GQDs@PtxPb with core-shell structure, which is composed of PtxPb alloy shell, GQDs interlayer, and Au core without using any capping agents. In brief, Au@GQDs was firstly prepared through reducing HAuCl4 by sodium citrate at 100 °C. Then ascorbic acid (AA) was served to reduce H2PtCl6 in the presence of Au@GQDs to obtain the Au@GQDs@Pt dendrite, it further served as dendrite seeds to synthesize the final products. The as-prepared Au@GQDs@PtxPb display uniform morphology like nano-pompons and its electro-catalytic performance for MOR was carefully investigated. Interestingly, the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb exhibited brilliant MOR performance in the alkaline medium. Meanwhile, the durability of the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb was much better than commercial Pt/C. The synthesis process of Au@GQDs@PtxPb is shown in Scheme 1 .

Graphite was purchased from Shanpu Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sulfuric acid (H2SO4) and nitric acid (HNO3) were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium citrate dihydrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O), Pb(NO3)2 was supplied by the Tianjin Kaixin Chemical Reagent Company. Gold chloride tri-hydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O) and Chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6·6H2O) were subscribed from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Pt/C (20%) was ordered from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. All used chemicals were of analytical grade and without further decontamination. Twice-distilled water was used throughout the work.

The production of GQDs was synthesized through a modified nano-cutting method according to a published paper [39]. In a typical synthetic method of the GQDs, 20 mL HNO3 and 60 mL H2SO4 were added into 300 mg graphite powder, and then the solution was ultra-sonically for about 0.5 h to developing a uniform solution. The concoction was continuously reacting for 12 h at 120 °C in a 100 mL round-bottom flask. When cooling to the normal temperature, the mixture was neutralized with 300 mL double distilled water and Na2CO3. Afterward, a 220 nm filter membrane was employed to remove the Na2SO4 and NaNO3 at −4 °C. Subsequently, the semi-finished products were dialyzed by a dialysis bag (3500 Da) for 48 h.

Au@GQDs was produced via reducing HAuCl4·3H2O with the help of sodium citrate following the predecessors [40]. For a short time, the Au@GQDs were obtained by injecting 200 μL of tetrachloroauric acid (HAuCl4·3H2O, 25.4 mM) into a mixed solution containing 200 μL GQDs and 35 mL H2O, afterward, 200 μL sodium citrate (0.1 M) was injected instantly when 100 °C, then react for 0.5 h with continuous stirred. Thus, the solution turned to claret-red within 30 min, indicative the formation of Au@GQDs. Later, Au@GQDs was collected as a precipitate.

To prepare Au@GQDs@Pt materials, 120 μL H2PtCl6·6H2O (19.3 mM) was injected into the Au@GQDs (10 mL) and then incubating for 2 min. Ascorbic acid (AA, 500 μL, 0.1 M), which acts as a reducing agent, was put into the reactants at 43 °C. The liquid was responding for 55 min, when it changed to blue-brown, then the Au@GQDs@Pt were obtained by centrifugation, and denoted as Au@GQDs@Pt1Pb0.

After the Au@GQDs@Pt1Pb0 were centrifugal purified, 10 mL double-distilled water containing the desired stoichiometric amounts of Pb(NO3)2, and 500 μL AA (0.1 M) was injected into the above mixture, then the reaction was reserved for 1 h at 55 °C. The products were centrifuged, washed with ethanol/water mixtures, and re-dispersed in double-distilled water for further use. The obtained samples are denoted as Au@GQDs@Pt2.3Pb, Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb, Au@GQDs@Pt6.9Pb, they were all synthesized with a parallel process apart from the quantity of Pb(NO3)2 was altered to 40 μL, 80 μL, and 120 μL (8.39 mM).

Au@Pt3.5Pb, GQDs@Pt3.5Pb, and commercial Pt/C were used for comparison. Au@Pt3.5Pb was manufactured by the same method as Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb except for the addition of GQDs. GQDs@Pt3.5Pb was produced via a similar method as Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb apart from the addition of Au. To formulate the Pt/C ink, 8.9 mg 20 wt% Pt/C was dissolved in 2 mL aqueous solution with ultrasonic.

The morphology and structure of the products were studied through electron microscopy operating at 200 kV. High-angle annular dark-field (HAADF-STEM) joined with energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) were hired to detect the elemental distribution of the materials. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was accompanied by a PHI-5702 (USA). Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were applied to illustrate the alloying features. UV–visible spectra were obtained with a MAPADA P4 instrument in the span of 200–800 nm.

To evaluate the performance of the materials towards the MOR, electrochemical experiments were performed in an ordinary 3-electrode linked to a CHI660 electrochemical workstation purchased from the Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Company. The calomel electrode, Pt wire, and glassy‑carbon electrode (GCE, 0.07065 cm2) functioned as the reference electrode, counter electrode, and the working electrode, respectively. Before the dropping of samples, the GCE electrodes were smoothed carefully with 0.3, and 0.05 μm alumina powder. After that, the electrodes were accepted to dry with nitrogen. 5 μL of the ink was deposited on a pre-cleaned GCE surface, after that, the catalyst electrode was obtained.

The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) was reckoned by integrating the hydrogen adsorption charge on cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves at room temperature in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Methanol electro-oxidation activity of the synthesis catalysts was tested in 1 M NaOH + 0.5 M CH3OH solution. The chronoamperometry (CA) method were conducted to estimate the long-term stability of the catalyst, it recorded at −0.2 V for 3000 s. All of the electrochemical experiments were performed at room temperature with the potential scan rate of 50 mV s−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb core-shell pompons
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and structure of the as-prepared GQDs and Au@GQDs were characterized by electron microscopy (TEM and HR-TEM). As displayed in Fig. 1(a), GQDs present homogeneous dispersal with Gaussian size distribution and an average size of 4.14±0.62 nm. Moreover, in Fig. 1(b), one clear absorption band was observed at 234 nm in the UV–visible spectra, which are correlated to the π→π* transitions of C=C bonds [41]. An additional shoulder peak at 265 nm was assigned to the n→π* electron transition of heteroatoms (C=O), it was further proved the establishment of GQDs [42].

After the successful preparation of GQDs, the precursor (HAuCl4·3H2O) was injected into the GQDs to form the Au@GQDs. The diameter of Au@GQDs is about 16.68±1.08 nm (inset of Fig. 1c), and the outer graphene shell with a thickness of 1.17 nm. Moreover, the HR-TEM of Au@GQDs displays three different lattices of 0.235 nm, 0.203 nm, and 0.238 nm in the internal and peripheral areas (Fig. 1d), which agrees to the (111), (200) planes of Au and (100) plane of graphite, respectively [43,44]. In the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) pattern (inset of Fig. 1d), the diffuse ring agreeing to the typical carbon, and the ordered diffraction spots connected to the Au are observed [45]. The diffraction rings in SAED could be effortlessly located to the (111), (220), and (200) planes of the Au (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). As already stated, the results of HR-TEM, FFT, and SAED are all indicating the successful synthetic of Au@GQDs, which are consistent with the consequences of XPS. It also proves that the as-synthesized Au@GQDs was functionalized by various carbon-containing groups and oxygen-containing groups. The plentiful groups are not only competent to reduce Au3+ ions into Au0 for the production of Au nano-particle (Au NPs) but also help the effective encapsulating of Au NPs with GQDs coating via hydrogen-bonding. They all enable the Au@GQDs nano-composite brilliant monodispersity and stability [46,47].

To approved the Au@GQDs@Pt (Au@GQDs@Pt1Pb0) with dendritic structure. Following on, the TEM and HR-TEM of the hierarchical Au@GQDs@Pt are shown in Fig. 1(e), (f). The medium-magnification TEM image shows that the material includes an abundantly nano-dendrites shell with uniform distribution, which revealed that several Pt dendritic tendrils had grown on the Au@GQDs nanoparticle instead of a compact Pt shell. The diameter of Au@GQDs@Pt ranges in sizes of 31.14±2.98 nm is shown in the histogram (inset in Fig. 1e). Additionally, a high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image (Fig. 1f) reveals several easy identified lattice distances with 0.235 nm, 0.225 nm, and 0.196 nm, which are agreeing with the (111) facets of face-centered cubic (fcc) Au and (200), (111) facets of Pt, respectively [48,49]. These indications powerfully confirmed that the successful preparation of the Au@GQDs@Pt core-shell nano-sphere with abundant dendritic. Moreover, their crystal characteristic was also approved by the SAED image (inset Fig. 2f). Meanwhile, the EDX spectrum displayed the distinct Pt signal, also suggesting that Pt has grown on the Au@GQDs surface (Fig. S2).

To enhance the performance of Au@GQDs@Pt for the MOR, we employed it to serve as both morphological and compositional templates to synthesize the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb with rich open channels. In this strategy, the shape, and dispersion are significantly easier to control than one-pot method (Fig.S3). Therefore, we chose two-step reduction method to synthesize the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb. Fig. 1(g), (h) further study the morphology and crystal structure of Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb nano-pompons synthesized by two-step reduction method. Fig. 1(g) clearly shows that the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb microspheres have a uniform diameter of about 32.6±3.3 nm with the Au@GQDs core of about 16±1 nm, and the shell possesses abundant channels running through the pompons. These open structures can provide more available surface, and fast mass transport efficiency, consequently, accelerating the reaction rate of the catalysts [50]. The HR-TEM image (Fig. 1h) reveals lattice spaces of 0.235 Å matching it to the Au (111) plane of the density core, and the gray shell with lattice spaces of 0.233 Å and 0.209 Å agreeing to the (111) and (110) planes of the PtxPb alloy [51]. After careful examination, we did not find any independent lattice of Pb or Pt. They all powerfully proved that the realization of the Pt3.5Pb alloy shell. Additionally, its crystal characteristic was defined by the SAED pattern and X-ray diffraction (XRD). SAED shows the bright and discrete diffraction spots (inset in Fig. 1h), that can be pointed to the planes of (311), (220), (200), (100), (101), and (111), which is in harmony with the XRD image.

To understand the conversion mechanism of Pt dendritic to PtPb alloy shell, the growth intermediates were collected and investigated by TEM images as shown in Fig. S4. Pt dendritic nanostructures, before the injection of Pb precursor, are shown in Fig. 1 (e, f). With Pb precursor injected, the intermediates at 10 min reaction (Fig. S4 (b)) contain the d-spacing of 2.33 Å for Pt3.5Pb (111), and 2.86 Å corresponding to (111) crystal planes of fcc-Pb (JCPDS no. 04–0686), indicating that the phases of intermediates are changed to both PtxPb alloy and Pb nanoparticles. Further, increase to 30 min, the surface structure contains (111) crystal planes of fcc-Pt, hexagonal close-packed (hcp) PtPb alloy (JCPDS no. 06–0374), and fcc-PtxPb alloy (JCPDS no. 06–0574), indicating the presence of the fcc-Pt, PtxPb, and PtPb alloy mix phase in the intermediates [51]. After 60 min, the Pt and Pb phase component completely disappeared, leading to the formation of the alloy phase as shown in Fig. S4(d). To see whether the phases have changed from Pt to PtxPb alloy on the dendritic Pt nanostructure, FFT patterns of reaction intermediates were also obtained as shown in Fig. S4. After 60 min, the FFT patterns for the shell are poorly defined due to the polycrystalline nature of the PtxPb alloy shell (Fig. S4 d), but the FFT patterns before the Pb precursor injected was well defined (Fig. S4 a). Based on the above results, the formation process of PtxPb alloy shell can be interpreted as conversion chemistry methods, which are also used to convert Pt into PtBi, PtPb, PtSn, and Pt3Fe via reaction with appropriate metal salt precursors under reducing conditions [12,52].

XRD was also conducted to determine the phases of the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb nano-pompons. As shown in Fig. 2(a), a wide diffraction peak was discovered in the span of 20 °-30 °, it could be indexed to the corresponding (002) plane of graphite for GQDs, due to sp2 hybridized carbon atoms, and the broadening is due to the formation of quantum dots. Meanwhile, the peaks at 36.5°, 42.5°, 45.5°, 66.1°, 71.7°, 75.5°, and 78.9° were corresponding with the PtxPb alloy (JCPDS card no. 06–0574, in green), and PtPb alloy (JCPDS no. 06–0374, blue), demonstrating the existence of both the PtxPb and PtPb alloy phase in the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb core-shell pompons. All the peaks were shifted to higher 2θ values, and the diffraction peak appears between standard peaks of Pt (JCPDS no. 04–0802) and Pb (JCPDS no. 04–0686), further demonstrating that Pt and Pb produced alloys in the shell.

Moreover, the line-scanning profile (Fig. 2b) of the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb further reveals the elemental composition and distribution of Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb. As a result, this is directly confirmed the formation of Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb core-shell structure, again. Also, EDX (Fig. 2c) measurement confirm that this material contains elements of C, Au, Pt, and Pb, which is in agreement with the line-scanning profile and HAADF-STEM results. The core-shell alloy structures of Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb were also approved by HAADF-STEM shown in Fig. 2(f), which revealed that the individual nano-pompon was composed of elements Au (green), Pt (yellow), and Pb (blue). The element Au mainly made the core, while Pt and Pb elements are mixed relatively uniformly, implying that Pt and Pb elements may form an alloy, which is further confirmed by the overlay of the mapping images of all three elements.

Additionally, the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb core-shell framework construction was proved by the UV–visible spectrum (Fig. S5). That displayed no peaks comparing to Au@GQDs, which exposed a typical absorption peak situated at 522 nm, which is attributed to the surface plasmon of Au. This is alike to the UV–visible spectrum of Au-Pt core-shell material [53], revealing the suppression of the Au band by Pt3.5Pb alloy shells.

To further survey the chemical valence state and electronic structure of as-synthesized catalysts, we used the XPS instrument to explore the materials. The presence of Pt, Pb, Au, C, and O were confirmed in the XPS survey of the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb sample (Fig. 3a), which is in agreement with the EDX, also demonstrating that the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb nano-catalyst is successfully prepared. The C1s spectra can be de-convoluted into three peaks highlighted at 284.76, 286.10, and 289 eV, which stand for the sp2 carbon (C=C) in graphene, the sp3 carbon in C-O, and the O-C=O in carboxyl group, respectively (Fig. 3b), indicating that GQDs has been successfully transformed into Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb. The de-convolution of O1s also generated two peaks showing at 531.26 and 532.64 eV with the prior being ascribed to C=O/-OH, and the second to C-O/C-O-C groups (Fig. 3c). As well, the high-resolution Au can be tailored into two peaks noticed at 84.01 eV, which is assigned to Au 4f7/2, and the peak at 87.64 eV represent Au 4f5/2 [54]. The XPS high-resolution of Pt 4f (Fig. 3e) shows two chief peaks traced at 74.64 eV and 71.39 eV, that are endorsed to the Pt 4f7/2 and Pt 4f5/2 of Pt(0) state, while the two trifling signals founded at 72.51 eV and 76.39 eV match up to the Pt 4f7/2 and Pt 4f5/2 of Pt2+ [55]. Additionally, we can observe that a similar value of Pt(0), however, the Pt2+ peak position of Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb is shifted positively (0.7 eV) than that for the Au@GQDs@Pt. The positive binding energy (BE) of Pt 4f is ascribed to the re-modify of both d-band and sp-band of Pt atoms in the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb. Positive shifts of the BE in Pt atoms suggests the downshift of the d-band center and decreases the electron back-donation from Pt 5d orbital to the 2p* orbital of CO, thus causes the Pt 5d bands are partial filling by the increased electron density and the weakening of CO-Pt bonding [56], finally probably reducing the carbonaceous intermediate poisoning. For the dendritic Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb nano-pompons, the XPS data of Pb (137.01 for Pb 4f7/2, 141.76 for Pb 4f5/2) and Pb oxide types (138.89 for Pb 4f7/2, 143.64 for Pb 4f5/2) are likewise detected. The shifts of the binding energy also suggest that the electronic interaction between Pt and Pb, and further demonstrates the formation of PtxPb alloy, such bimetallic alloys in other reports also exhibit a similar changes in BE [16].

Inspired by their striking merits, the Au@GQDs@PtxPb materials with changing Pt/Pb proportions were assessed by electrochemical measurement methods to find the optimal proportion for MOR. Fig. 4a displays the obtained CV curves, are accustomed to calculate the electrochemically active surface areas (ECSA) of these materials.

The ECSA can be calculated from the subsequent Eq. [57]:
ECSA =
Q H
0.21 ×
M Pt

In the formula, QH is the charge for H2 sorption or desorption, and 0.21 mC cm−2 is the charge required to oxidize a monolayer of H2 on the surface of Pt, and MPt stands for the quantity of Pt, and the loadings are gauged by ICP-AES.

The ECSA of Au@GQDs@PtxPb electrodes with Pt/Pb mole ratios of 1:0, 2.3:1, 3.5:1, and 6.9:1 are 4.99 m2g−1, 7.40 m2g−1, 15.77 m2g−1, and 2.95 m2g−1, respectively (Fig. 4a). Remarkably enough, Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb have the maximum ECSA of 15.77 m2g−1, indicating that a proper Pt/Pb (3.5:1) could permit the catalyst performance more available. Meanwhile, the CV curves were also recorded in 1.0 M NaOH solution to calculate ECSA by the same method, Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb also showed the biggest active surface areas (Fig. S6).

Generally, the mass activity (MA), specific activity (SA), anti-poisoning capability, oxidation potential, and stability also are specified to estimate MOR electro-catalytic property. Therefore, the methanol oxidation examinations were then performed in 1 M NaOH + 0.5 M methanol. The potential on Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb (−0.54 V) is shifted to more negative than Au@GQDs@Pt1Pb0 (−0.48 V), Au@GQDs@Pt2.3Pb (−0.44 V), and Au@GQDs@Pt6.9Pb (−0.44 V), which implies that the MOR might be trending to initiate at a suitable potential in the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb nano-pompons.

In the meantime, the peak current density confirms that the MOR activity of Au@GQDs@PtxPb rises with the increase of Pt proportion, and the optimal current (98.86 mA cm−2) was attained when Pt/Pb molar ratio is 3.5:1, which ascribe to an equilibrium between the channel-rich organization and appropriate Pb content, thus enabled the advanced catalyst activity, which is 37.88 times as high as commercial Pt/C. But when the quantity of Pb is continue amplified, the MOR activity on Au@GQDs@Pt6.9Pb displays a descent, which ascribed to overcapacity Pb element swapping some outside Pt active sites.

Moreover, the electrochemical measurement results visibly manifest that the quality of Pb could improve the MA and SA in Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb compared to Au@GQDs@Pt1Pb0 (Au@GQDs@Pt). It further confirmed that the presence of Pb in the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb played a crucial role in enhancing the electro-catalytic activity. The incorporation of oxophilic metal Pb can dissociate water to form oxygenated species such as OHads. Such oxygenated species can promote the further oxidation of COads, and then remove them from Pt sites, which substantially enhance the catalytic properties of Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb. Moreover, the Pt electronic structure was modified by the introduction of Pb, which leads to the surface of PtxPb has lower d-band energy than the surface of Pt, thereby enhanced the catalytic activity of Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb.

The chronoamperometric tests were employed to assess the stability of the catalysts, which was accomplished for a period of 3000 s in 1 M NaOH + 0.5 M methanol solution at −0.2 V, as shown in Fig. 4(d). After the 3000 s tests, the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb still displays the maximum current (6.74 mA cm−2) compared to the Au@GQDs@Pt1Pb0 (0.8 mA cm−2), Au@GQDs@Pt2.3Pb (1.3 mA cm−2), and Au@GQDs@Pt6.9Pb (0.4 mA cm−2) nano-catalysts, further confirmed that the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb nano-catalyst owns the long-term electrochemical durability for MOR in alkaline solutions. The morphological and composition change of the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb after the durability tests were characterized by TEM and ICP-AES. There was almost no significant change of the nano-pompons structure and composition of the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb (Fig. S7 and Table S1). It further highlights the excellent durability of the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb nano-pompons.

In brief, if the mole ratio of Pt: Pb is set as 3.5:1, the catalytic activity and stability are excellent, so the proportion was selected to compare with Au@Pt3.5Pb, GQDs@Pt3.5Pb, and commercial Pt/C to explore the role of GQDs, and Au in MOR. As exhibited in Fig. 5(a), the ECSA of Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb nano-pompons, Au@Pt3.5Pb, GQDs@Pt3.5Pb, and commercial Pt/C were calculated to be 15.77m2 g−1, 8.59 m2 g−1, 0.95 m2 g−1, and 0.44 m2 g−1, respectively. As expected, the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb catalyst shows extraordinary ECSA towards MOR, most likely due to the channel-rich structure of nano-pompons, which has clarified by the TEM.

As revealed in Fig. 5 (b), it is seen that the onset potential for the MOR on the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb catalyst is significantly shifted to more negative regions, figuring out that the catalytic reactions are easier to be initialized than the Pt/C catalyst. In comparison with Au@Pt3.5Pb, the peak current of Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb (98.86 mA cm−2) is almost 2.5 times higher than that for Au@Pt3.5Pb (39.14 mA cm−2). The results displayed that GQDs play a vital role in enhancing the catalytic performance of the material. The following three aspects can illuminate this role: (1) The GQDs are anticipated to exhibit abundant structural defects, which can accelerate the electron transfer between the Pt and electrolyte, thus enhance the electronic conductivity, as suggested in a recent theoretical study [35]; (2) Also, the existence of GQDs can provide a fast pathway for the activation of water, thus speeding up the conversion rate of chemisorbed CO to CO2, and improve its catalytic ability in methanol and anti-COads poisoning [37]; (3) The GQDs can deliver an abundance of nucleating and anchoring sites, which promotes the formation of Pt nano-dendrites and confines the agglomeration of Pt particles, thus it can provide more surface area and active sites [38].

To explore the role of the Au core in MOR, the CVs of GQDs@Pt3.5Pb were recorded under identical conditions for comparison. The Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb catalyst exhibited 5.86 times catalyze activity than GQDs@Pt3.5Pb (16.86 mA cm−2), it also presents outstanding durability than GQDs@Pt3.5Pb. Thus, electrochemical tests have verified that the introduction of an Au core could improve the catalytic activity and durability of electro-catalysts, which ascribed to the Au can increase the amount of surface-active OH− by the adsorption of active oxygen species, promoting the interactions between OH− and poisonous species [23].

The Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb similarly displays maximum MA and SA (Fig. 5c). In particular, the catalytic performance of the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb towards the MOR in alkaline electrolyte had been prominently improved than commercial Pt/C and most state-of-the-art recorded Pt-based catalysts (Table 1 ). Besides the electro-catalytic activity, the electro-catalytic stability of the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb was superior to 20% Pt/C as well. After the chronoamperometric tests at −0.2 V continue 3000 s (Fig. 5d), Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb catalyst displayed the unhurried current decay among other catalysts, which should be attributed to the channel-riched Pt3.5Pb alloys shells as well as the stability of core-shell structure with the help of Au@GQDs.

The advanced reaction kinetic and excellent current of the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb in alkaline methanol might be explained by the possible theory degradation mechanisms in DMFC as the following process (Eqs. (1)–(6)) [51].

In alkaline CH3OH, the OHads are essential groups that facilitate the H subtraction procedure of MOR. OHads can be produced on Pb atoms through the discharge of OH−. The establishment of Pb-OHads (Eqs. 2) could be enhanced on the surface of the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb core-shell nano-pompons and promote the MOR reaction. Furthermore, the Pb-OHads can respond with byproduct to recover Pt active sites, which can adsorb the CH3OH, again. In summary, Pb atoms can promote the progress of dehydrogenation, meanwhile, the diverse pathways of MOR result in the complete Pt-(CH3O)ads oxidation and prevent producing much COads immersed on the catalyst surface.

Combine the theoretical study with the experimental on the MOR, the advanced catalytic performance of the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb than commercial Pt/C in the alkaline electrolyte can be attributed to the thereinafter aspects: (i) Owing to the weaker electronegativity of Pb, and Pt3.5Pb alloy modified the electronic cloud density of Pt, these can boost the adsorption of OH− and under similar situations, it can also decline the adsorption energy of the Pt-COads and facilitate the oxidation of COads. Therefore, the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb can excellently avoid being affected by COads and promote CH3COads oxide. (ii) The inner Au@GQDs like a freeway accelerate the electron transport, also play an important role to preserve the core-shell construction when operation for a long-time, thereby enhancing the stability of the catalyst. (iii) The pompons structure consists of rich open channels and branches, that can efficient exposure of catalytic active sites and inhibit the aggregate of materials, and then could facilitate the electron transfer in different regions, thus speeding up the reaction rate of MOR.

In summary, a series of Au@GQDs@PtxPb nano-pompons have been successfully fabricated by a convenient and green strategy without adding any capping agent and stabilizer. As a result, the optimized Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb catalyst exhibits better MOR activity, anti-CO poisoning, and stability than Au@GQDs@Pt, and commercial Pt/C. The onset potential on the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb catalyst (−0.54 V) starts much earlier than commercial Pt/C (−0.44 V) catalysts, and the Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb exhibits the highest specific activity of 98.86 mA cm−2, which is 37.88 times higher than commercial Pt/C (2.61 mA cm−2), respectively. The outstanding performance of Au@GQDs@Pt3.5Pb catalyst is due to its well-defined core-shell nano-pompons structure, and their favorable composition.
